# Gemini Review Pass 1: Fact Checker (RAG-Grounded)

> **You are a fact-checking reviewer for Ukrainian language curriculum.**
> Your ONLY task: compare the module content against authoritative reference sources.
> You do NOT evaluate style, pedagogy, or naturalness — that is a separate reviewer's job.

**IMPORTANT: This content was generated by another AI agent. You are an independent fact-checker.
Compare claims against the provided reference sources. Do NOT evaluate from your own knowledge.**

---

## Module Content

```markdown
You've learned all the letters of the Ukrainian alphabet, and you can break words into syllables. Now it's time to discover the **music** of Ukrainian — where to place the beat in a word, and how your voice rises and falls in a sentence.

By the end of this module, you'll be able to spot the stressed syllable in common words, understand why stress matters so much in Ukrainian, and use the right melody for statements, questions, and exclamations.

## Наголос — Stress

Every Ukrainian word has one syllable that you say a little louder and longer than the others. This emphasis is called **наго́лос** (stress). In English, you already do this naturally — think of the difference between *REcord* (a noun) and *reCORD* (a verb). Ukrainian works the same way, but with an important twist.

In some languages, stress follows a predictable pattern. Polish puts the stress on the second-to-last syllable, almost without exception. French stresses the final syllable. Ukrainian has no such rule. Stress in Ukrainian is **free** — it can land on any syllable of the word. There is no shortcut, no formula. You simply learn the stress for each word when you learn the word itself.

Why does this matter? Because in Ukrainian, stress can change the meaning of a word entirely. Look at these two words — they are spelled with the exact same letters:

- **за́мок** — a castle
- **замо́к** — a lock

Same spelling, completely different meanings. Here's another pair:

- **му́ка** — torment, suffering
- **мука́** — flour

<!-- adapted from: Avramenko, Grade 5, p.88 — замок minimal pair exercise -->

If you stress the wrong syllable, you might say «flour» when you mean «torment» — or talk about a lock when you mean a castle! These word pairs are called **minimal pairs** — words that differ only in stress placement.

[!tip] **How to read stress marks**
In Ukrainian dictionaries and textbooks, stress is shown with an accent mark (´) over the stressed vowel: **вода́**, **мо́лодь**, **за́мок**. Whenever you look up a new word, pay attention to this little mark — it tells you exactly where to place the beat.

Here's your most important strategy as a learner: when you encounter a new Ukrainian word, **always check where the stress falls**. Don't guess from the spelling — Ukrainian spelling does not reveal the stress. Look it up, listen to it, and say it out loud a few times.

<!-- adapted from: Vashulenko, Grade 2, p.16 — "Щоб запам'ятати наголос у слові, правильно проговори його кілька разів." -->

[!note] **Stress mark reminder**
You already know that Ukrainian vowels are always pronounced clearly and fully, whether stressed or not. There is no vowel reduction like in English or Russian. But the stressed vowel is still louder and slightly longer — that's how a listener knows which syllable carries the **наго́лос**.

## Типові наголоси — Common Stress Patterns

While there is no single rule for Ukrainian stress, you'll start to notice tendencies as your vocabulary grows. Let's look at three common patterns.

**First-syllable stress** appears in many basic, everyday words — especially short family and household words you already know:

- **ма́ма** — mom
- **та́то** — dad
- **ха́та** — house
- **ка́ва** — coffee
- **до́брий** — good

You've been saying **ма́ма** and **та́то** since your very first module. The stress sits right on the first syllable — nice and comfortable.

**Last-syllable stress** is common in longer words and some verb forms:

- **молоко́** — milk
- **далеко́** — far
- **вода́** — water
- **мука́** — flour

Notice how all four words end with the stress on the very last syllable. This pattern is especially frequent in three-syllable words.

**Penultimate stress** (second-to-last syllable) shows up frequently in two- and three-syllable words:

- **шко́ла** — school
- **кни́жка** — book
- **доро́га** — road

Here's the tricky part: the same ending can carry different stress. Both **кни́га** (book) and **вода́** (water) end in **-а**, but the stress falls on different syllables. Both **шко́ла** and **далеко́** have an **о**, but the stress is in different places. This is why you cannot predict stress from spelling alone — you must learn it word by word.

[!practice] **Quick check**
Look at these words you already know. Can you feel where the stress falls?
- **мо́локо** — no! It's **молоко́** (last syllable)
- **ка́ва** — yes! First syllable
- **шко́ла** — yes! First syllable (penultimate in two syllables)

## Рухомий наголос — Mobile Stress

Here's something fascinating about Ukrainian stress — it doesn't just vary between different words. It can actually **move within the same word** when the word changes form. This is called **mobile stress**.

You haven't learned word forms yet (that's coming soon with cases and verb conjugation), but let's preview this concept so you're prepared.

Take the word **рука́** (hand, arm). When you use it in a different form, the stress shifts:

- **рука́** — hand (basic form)
- **ру́ки** — hands (plural form)

The stress jumped from the last syllable to the first! The word didn't change much in its letters, but the beat moved.

The same thing happens with verbs. Take **писа́ти** (to write):

- **писа́ти** — to write
- **пишу́** — I write
- **пи́шеш** — you write

The stress moved three times across three forms of the same verb! First it sat on the second syllable (**писа́ти**), then moved to the end (**пишу́**), then jumped back to the beginning (**пи́шеш**).

[!tip] **Don't worry — awareness is the goal**
You don't need to memorize all stress shifts right now. When you start learning cases and verb conjugation in future modules, you'll practice these patterns step by step. For now, just know that stress can move — and that's completely normal in Ukrainian.

The best way to internalize mobile stress patterns is by **listening to native speakers**. Pay attention to how they pronounce words in different forms. Your ear will start picking up the patterns naturally, even before you learn the grammar rules. Every time you hear a word like **вода́**, try repeating it out loud: **вода́**. Then later, when you hear **во́ди** (of water), notice how the stress shifted. This kind of active listening is your secret weapon.

[!culture] **Ukrainian castles**
Remember **за́мок** (castle)? Ukraine has stunning medieval castles — the fortress at Кам'яне́ць-Поді́льський and the Оле́ський за́мок are among the most famous. When Ukrainians talk about their castles, they always say **за́мок** with stress on the first syllable. If they mean the lock on their door, it's **замо́к** — stress on the last syllable.

## Інтонація — Intonation

Now let's move from individual words to whole sentences. **Інтона́ція** (intonation) is the melody of a sentence — how your pitch rises and falls as you speak.

Ukrainian has four basic intonation patterns that you'll use every **день** (day). Let's explore them using one simple phrase: **Це кафе́** (This is a café).

**Declarative (statement):** Your pitch gently falls at the end of the sentence.

> **Це кафе́.** ↘

You're simply stating a fact — that's a café. Your voice goes down at the end, like a calm landing. This is the same pattern you use in English for statements.

**Question with a question word:** Your pitch rises on the question word, then falls.

> **Де́ кафе́?** ↗↘

The word **де** (where) gets a little lift, and then your voice settles down. The **пита́ння** (question) word carries the main energy.

**Yes/no question (without a question word):** Your pitch rises sharply at the end.

> **Це кафе́?** ↗

This is the trickiest pattern for English speakers! In English, most questions rise at the end. In Ukrainian, only yes/no questions do this. The **відпо́відь** (answer) to this kind of question is **так** or **ні**.

**Exclamatory:** Your voice rises with strong emphasis.

> **Це кафе́!** ↗!

You're surprised or excited — maybe you've found your favorite café! The pitch rises with energy and emotion.

[!warning] **Common English-speaker mistake**
English speakers tend to raise their pitch at the end of *all* questions. In Ukrainian, only yes/no questions rise at the end. When you use a question word like **де** or **що**, the pitch rises on that word and then **falls**. Practice this contrast — it makes a big difference in how natural you sound.

Let's practice all four patterns with another sentence. Try reading each one out loud, paying attention to your pitch:

> **Це шко́ла.** ↘ (statement: This is a school.)
> **Де шко́ла?** ↗↘ (question with question word: Where is the school?)
> **Це шко́ла?** ↗ (yes/no question: Is this a school?)
> **Це шко́ла!** ↗! (exclamation: This is a school! / What a school!)

<!-- adapted from: Vashulenko, Grade 2, p.108-116 — intonation of different sentence types -->

And one more set with a word you know well — try saying «**до́брий ра́нок**» (good morning) as:

> **До́брий ра́нок.** ↘ (a calm greeting)
> **До́брий ра́нок?** ↗ (is it really a good morning?)
> **До́брий ра́нок!** ↗! (an enthusiastic greeting)

[!practice] **Contrast drill**
Read this phrase three ways. Feel how your voice changes each time:
1. **Це молоко́.** ↘ — You're pointing at milk on the table.
2. **Це молоко́?** ↗ — You're not sure if it's milk.
3. **Це молоко́!** ↗! — You're excited to see milk!

## Практика — Practice

You've learned a lot! Let's put it all together with some practice.

**Stress placement:** Read each word out loud and tap the table on the stressed syllable:

- **вода́** (tap on -да́)
- **шко́ла** (tap on шко́-)
- **молоко́** (tap on -ко́)
- **до́брий** (tap on до́-)
- **далеко́** (tap on -ко́)
- **рука́** (tap on -ка́)

**Minimal pairs:** Say both words in each pair. Can you hear the difference?

- **за́мок** (castle) — **замо́к** (lock)
- **му́ка** (torment) — **мука́** (flour)

**Intonation reading:** Read each sentence with the correct melody:

> **Це вода́.** ↘
> **Де вода́?** ↗↘
> **Це вода́?** ↗
> **Це вода́!** ↗!

Great job making it through all these exercises! Remember, you don't need to be perfect right away. Every time you practice, your ear and your voice get a little bit better.

## Підсумок — Summary

You've just unlocked the music of Ukrainian! Here's what you now know:

- Ukrainian **наго́лос** (stress) is **free** — it can fall on any syllable — and **mobile** — it can shift when a word changes form.
- Stress changes meaning: **за́мок** (castle) vs **замо́к** (lock).
- Ukrainian vowels stay clear and pure whether stressed or not — no mumbling!
- **Інтона́ція** (intonation) gives sentences their melody: pitch falls for statements ↘, rises at the end for yes/no questions ↗, and rises on the question word for wh-questions ↗↘.

**Self-check — test yourself:**

1. Where is the stress in **вода́**? (Answer: last syllable)
2. What happens to vowel quality when a vowel is unstressed? (Answer: nothing — Ukrainian vowels stay clear)
3. How does **Це кафе́?** ↗ differ from **Де кафе́?** ↗↘?

**Coming up next:** In Module 7, you'll learn your first greetings and basic phrases — **до́брий день**, **як спра́ви**, and more. The stress and intonation skills you've built here will help you sound natural from your very first conversation!
```

## Plan (Source of Truth for Scope)

```yaml
module: a1-006
level: A1
sequence: 6
slug: stress-and-intonation
version: '3.0'
title: Stress and Intonation
subtitle: The Music of Ukrainian
focus: grammar
pedagogy: PPP
phase: A1.1 [First Contact]
word_target: 1200
objectives:
- Identify the stressed syllable in common Ukrainian words
- Understand that Ukrainian stress is free and mobile
- Produce correct intonation for statements, questions, and exclamations
- Use stress marks when reading dictionary entries
content_outline:
- section: Наголос — Stress
  words: 350
  points:
  - 'Free and mobile stress concept: unlike Polish (penultimate) or French (final), Ukrainian stress can fall on any syllable
    — there is no fixed rule.'
  - 'Stress changes meaning: зАмок (castle) vs замОк (lock), мУка (torment) vs мукА (flour) — minimal pairs that demonstrate
    the functional load of stress.'
  - 'How stress is marked in dictionaries and textbooks: the acute accent (´) over the stressed vowel; practice reading dictionary
    entries.'
  - 'Learner strategy: when encountering a new word, always check stress placement — guessing from spelling will often be wrong.'
- section: Типові наголоси — Common Stress Patterns
  words: 250
  points:
  - 'First-syllable stress: мАма, тАто, хАта, кАва — common in basic family and household words.'
  - 'Last-syllable stress: молокО, далекО, говорЮ — common in longer words and some verb forms.'
  - 'Penultimate stress: шкОла, кнИжка, дорОга — frequent in two- and three-syllable words.'
  - 'No fixed rule: the same ending can have different stress (кнИга vs водА, both -а ending) — stress must be learned per word.'
- section: Рухомий наголос — Mobile Stress
  words: 250
  points:
  - 'Stress shifts in declension: рукА (nominative) → рУки (genitive/plural) — the stress moves when the word form changes.'
  - 'Stress shifts in conjugation: писАти (infinitive) → пишУ (I write) → пИшеш (you write) — verb stress can shift between
    stems and endings.'
  - 'Preview note: mobile stress will matter more when learning cases and verb conjugation — for now, awareness is the goal.'
  - 'Practical tip: listening to native speakers is the best way to internalize stress patterns.'
- section: Інтонація — Intonation
  words: 250
  points:
  - 'Declarative intonation: pitch falls at the end of the sentence — Це кафе. with a downward contour.'
  - 'Interrogative with question word: pitch rises on the question word, then falls — ДЕ кафе?'
  - 'Yes/no questions (without question word): pitch rises sharply at the end — Це кафе? with an upward contour.'
  - 'Exclamatory intonation: sharp rise with emphasis — Це кафе! expressing surprise or excitement.'
  - 'Contrast drill: practicing the same sentence with all four intonation patterns.'
- section: Практика — Practice
  words: 100
  points:
  - 'Stress placement drills: identify which syllable carries the stress in common words.'
  - 'Minimal pairs practice: distinguish words that differ only in stress (зАмок/замОк, мУка/мукА).'
  - 'Intonation reading exercises: read the same sentence as a statement, question, and exclamation.'
- section: "Підсумок — Summary"
  words: 100
  points:
  - "Recap: stress is free and mobile, vowel purity under stress, rising intonation
    for questions, stress minimal pairs."
  - "Self-check: Where is the stress in вода? What happens to vowel quality when
    unstressed? How does question intonation differ from statement?"
  - "Next: M7 — greetings and basic phrases."
vocabulary_hints:
  required:
  - 'замок (castle/lock) — stress minimal pair: зАмок (castle) vs замОк (lock)'
  - 'вода (water) — last-syllable stress водА; Top 300 word; collocations: пити воду, холодна вода'
  - 'рука (hand/arm) — mobile stress рукА → рУки; Top 200 word'
  - писати (to write) — mobile stress in conjugation писАти → пишУ → пИшеш; Top 200 word
  - 'школа (school) — penultimate stress шкОла; Top 200 word'
  - 'молоко (milk) — last-syllable stress молокО'
  - 'добрий (good) — first-syllable stress дОбрий; Top 100 word; collocations: добрий день'
  recommended:
  - 'далеко (far) — last-syllable stress далекО'
  - наголос (stress/accent) — metalinguistic term
  - інтонація (intonation) — metalinguistic term
  - 'питання (question) — high-frequency word'
  - 'відповідь (answer) — high-frequency word'
activity_hints:
- type: quiz
  focus: Identify which syllable carries the stress
  items: 12
- type: match-up
  focus: Match stress minimal pairs to their meanings
  items: 8
- type: true-false
  focus: Recognize correct intonation patterns for sentence types
  items: 8
- type: fill-in
  focus: Place stress marks on common words
  items: 10
connects_to:
- a1-07 (The Gender Code)
prerequisites:
- a1-05 (Syllables and Word Division)
persona:
  voice: Patient Supportive Tutor
  role: Orchestra Conductor
grammar:
- Word stress
- Stress mobility
- Intonation patterns
register: розмовний
```

## Activities YAML

```yaml
- type: quiz
  title: Where Does the Stress Fall?
  instruction: Choose the syllable that carries the stress in each word.
  items:
  - question: Where is the stress in the word вода (water)?
    options:
    - text: First syllable (во-)
      correct: false
    - text: Last syllable (-да)
      correct: true
    - text: Both syllables equally
      correct: false
    - text: Ukrainian words have no stress
      correct: false
    explanation: 'Вода has last-syllable stress: вода́.'
  - question: Where is the stress in the word мама (mom)?
    options:
    - text: First syllable (ма-)
      correct: true
    - text: Last syllable (-ма)
      correct: false
    - text: Both syllables equally
      correct: false
    - text: It changes every time
      correct: false
    explanation: 'Мама has first-syllable stress: ма́ма.'
  - question: Where is the stress in the word молоко (milk)?
    options:
    - text: First syllable (мо-)
      correct: false
    - text: Second syllable (-ло-)
      correct: false
    - text: Last syllable (-ко)
      correct: true
    - text: Second and third equally
      correct: false
    explanation: 'Молоко has last-syllable stress: молоко́.'
  - question: Where is the stress in the word школа (school)?
    options:
    - text: First syllable (шко-)
      correct: true
    - text: Last syllable (-ла)
      correct: false
    - text: Both syllables equally
      correct: false
    - text: There is no stress
      correct: false
    explanation: 'Школа has first-syllable stress (penultimate): шко́ла.'
  - question: Where is the stress in the word далеко (far)?
    options:
    - text: First syllable (да-)
      correct: false
    - text: Second syllable (-ле-)
      correct: false
    - text: Last syllable (-ко)
      correct: true
    - text: First and last equally
      correct: false
    explanation: 'Далеко has last-syllable stress: далеко́.'
  - question: Where is the stress in the word добрий (good)?
    options:
    - text: First syllable (до-)
      correct: true
    - text: Last syllable (-рий)
      correct: false
    - text: Both syllables
      correct: false
    - text: No stress in this word
      correct: false
    explanation: 'Добрий has first-syllable stress: до́брий.'
  - question: Where is the stress in the word книжка (book)?
    options:
    - text: Last syllable (-ка)
      correct: false
    - text: First syllable (кни-)
      correct: true
    - text: Both syllables equally
      correct: false
    - text: The middle sound -ж-
      correct: false
    explanation: 'Книжка has first-syllable stress: кни́жка.'
  - question: Where is the stress in the word рука (hand)?
    options:
    - text: First syllable (ру-)
      correct: false
    - text: Last syllable (-ка)
      correct: true
    - text: Both equally
      correct: false
    - text: It has no stress
      correct: false
    explanation: 'Рука has last-syllable stress: рука́.'
  - question: Where is the stress in the word дорога (road)?
    options:
    - text: First syllable (до-)
      correct: false
    - text: Second syllable (-ро-)
      correct: true
    - text: Last syllable (-га)
      correct: false
    - text: First and second equally
      correct: false
    explanation: 'Дорога has penultimate stress: доро́га.'
  - question: Where is the stress in the word кава (coffee)?
    options:
    - text: First syllable (ка-)
      correct: true
    - text: Last syllable (-ва)
      correct: false
    - text: Both equally
      correct: false
    - text: No stress in short words
      correct: false
    explanation: 'Кава has first-syllable stress: ка́ва.'
  - question: Where is the stress in the word хата (house)?
    options:
    - text: Last syllable (-та)
      correct: false
    - text: First syllable (ха-)
      correct: true
    - text: Both syllables
      correct: false
    - text: It depends on the sentence
      correct: false
    explanation: 'Хата has first-syllable stress: ха́та.'
  - question: Where is the stress in the word тато (dad)?
    options:
    - text: First syllable (та-)
      correct: true
    - text: Last syllable (-то)
      correct: false
    - text: Both equally
      correct: false
    - text: No fixed stress
      correct: false
    explanation: 'Тато has first-syllable stress: та́то.'
- type: match-up
  title: Stress Minimal Pairs
  instruction: Match each stressed word to its correct English meaning. Pay attention
    to where the stress falls!
  pairs:
  - left: за́мок (stress on ЗА-)
    right: castle
  - left: замо́к (stress on -МОК)
    right: lock
  - left: му́ка (stress on МУ-)
    right: torment
  - left: мука́ (stress on -КА)
    right: flour
  - left: рука́ (stress on -КА)
    right: hand (singular)
  - left: ру́ки (stress on РУ-)
    right: hands (plural)
  - left: писа́ти (stress on -СА-)
    right: to write
  - left: пишу́ (stress on -ШУ)
    right: I write
- type: true-false
  title: Stress and Intonation Facts
  instruction: Decide whether each statement about Ukrainian stress and intonation
    is true or false.
  items:
  - statement: In Ukrainian, stress always falls on the first syllable of a word.
    correct: false
    explanation: Ukrainian stress is free — it can fall on any syllable. There is
      no fixed rule.
  - statement: The words за́мок (castle) and замо́к (lock) are spelled with the same
      letters but have different stress.
    correct: true
    explanation: These are stress minimal pairs — same spelling, different stress,
      different meaning.
  - statement: When you say a yes/no question in Ukrainian, your pitch rises at the
      end.
    correct: true
    explanation: Yes/no questions (without a question word) use rising intonation
      at the end.
  - statement: When you ask a question with де (where), your pitch rises at the end
      of the sentence.
    correct: false
    explanation: With a question word like де, the pitch rises on the question word
      and then falls.
  - statement: Ukrainian stress can move to a different syllable when a word changes
      form.
    correct: true
    explanation: This is called mobile stress. For example, рука́ becomes ру́ки in
      the plural.
  - statement: In a Ukrainian statement, your pitch rises at the end of the sentence.
    correct: false
    explanation: In statements, pitch gently falls at the end — like a calm landing.
  - statement: Unstressed Ukrainian vowels become unclear and mumbled, like in English.
    correct: false
    explanation: Ukrainian vowels stay clear and pure whether stressed or not — there
      is no vowel reduction.
  - statement: You can always predict Ukrainian stress from the spelling of a word.
    correct: false
    explanation: You cannot predict stress from spelling. Words like кни́га and вода́
      both end in -а but have different stress.
- type: fill-in
  title: Choose the Correct Stress
  instruction: Select the word with the stress mark on the correct syllable.
  items:
  - sentence: The word for 'water' with correct stress is ___.
    answer: вода́
    options:
    - во́да
    - вода́
    - вод́а
    - вода
    explanation: 'Вода has stress on the last syllable: вода́.'
  - sentence: The word for 'castle' with correct stress is ___.
    answer: за́мок
    options:
    - за́мок
    - замо́к
    - замоќ
    - замок
    explanation: Castle is за́мок with stress on the first syllable. Замо́к means
      lock.
  - sentence: The word for 'lock' with correct stress is ___.
    answer: замо́к
    options:
    - за́мок
    - замо́к
    - замоќ
    - замок
    explanation: Lock is замо́к with stress on the last syllable. За́мок means castle.
  - sentence: The word for 'milk' with correct stress is ___.
    answer: молоко́
    options:
    - мо́локо
    - моло́ко
    - молоко́
    - молоко
    explanation: 'Молоко has stress on the last syllable: молоко́.'
  - sentence: The word for 'school' with correct stress is ___.
    answer: шко́ла
    options:
    - шко́ла
    - школа́
    - школ́а
    - школа
    explanation: 'Школа has stress on the first syllable: шко́ла.'
  - sentence: The word for 'flour' with correct stress is ___.
    answer: мука́
    options:
    - му́ка
    - мука́
    - муќа
    - мука
    explanation: Flour is мука́ with stress on the last syllable. Му́ка means torment.
  - sentence: The word for 'coffee' with correct stress is ___.
    answer: ка́ва
    options:
    - ка́ва
    - кава́
    - кав́а
    - кава
    explanation: 'Кава has stress on the first syllable: ка́ва.'
  - sentence: The word for 'good' with correct stress is ___.
    answer: до́брий
    options:
    - до́брий
    - добри́й
    - добрий́
    - добрий
    explanation: 'Добрий has stress on the first syllable: до́брий.'
  - sentence: The word for 'far' with correct stress is ___.
    answer: далеко́
    options:
    - да́леко
    - дале́ко
    - далеко́
    - далеко
    explanation: 'Далеко has stress on the last syllable: далеко́.'
  - sentence: The word for 'road' with correct stress is ___.
    answer: доро́га
    options:
    - до́рога
    - доро́га
    - дорога́
    - дорога
    explanation: 'Дорога has stress on the second syllable: доро́га.'
- type: group-sort
  title: Sort Words by Stress Position
  instruction: Sort these words into groups based on which syllable carries the stress.
  groups:
  - name: First-syllable stress
    items:
    - ма́ма
    - та́то
    - ха́та
    - ка́ва
    - до́брий
  - name: Last-syllable stress
    items:
    - вода́
    - молоко́
    - далеко́
    - рука́
    - мука́
- type: quiz
  title: Intonation Patterns
  instruction: Choose the correct intonation pattern for each sentence type.
  items:
  - question: You say 'Це кафе.' as a simple statement. What does your pitch do?
    options:
    - text: Rises at the end
      correct: false
    - text: Falls at the end
      correct: true
    - text: Stays flat
      correct: false
    - text: Rises then falls then rises
      correct: false
    explanation: Statements use falling intonation at the end.
  - question: You ask 'Це кафе?' (Is this a cafe?) — a yes/no question. What does
      your pitch do?
    options:
    - text: Falls at the end
      correct: false
    - text: Stays flat
      correct: false
    - text: Rises sharply at the end
      correct: true
    - text: Falls then rises then falls
      correct: false
    explanation: Yes/no questions (without a question word) rise at the end.
  - question: You ask 'Де кафе?' (Where is the cafe?) with a question word. What does
      your pitch do?
    options:
    - text: Rises at the end
      correct: false
    - text: Rises on де, then falls
      correct: true
    - text: Falls the whole time
      correct: false
    - text: Stays flat throughout
      correct: false
    explanation: Questions with a question word rise on the question word, then fall.
  - question: You exclaim 'Це кафе!' with excitement. What does your pitch do?
    options:
    - text: Falls at the end
      correct: false
    - text: Stays flat
      correct: false
    - text: Falls then rises
      correct: false
    - text: Rises with strong emphasis
      correct: true
    explanation: Exclamations use rising pitch with energy and emphasis.
  - question: How is the intonation of 'Це школа?' different from 'Де школа?'
    options:
    - text: They use the same intonation
      correct: false
    - text: '''Це школа?'' rises at the end; ''Де школа?'' rises on де then falls'
      correct: true
    - text: '''Це школа?'' falls at the end; ''Де школа?'' rises at the end'
      correct: false
    - text: Both fall at the end
      correct: false
    explanation: Yes/no questions rise at the end. Questions with a question word
      rise on the question word and then fall.
  - question: An English speaker raises their pitch at the end of every question.
      Is this correct for Ukrainian?
    options:
    - text: Yes, Ukrainian works the same as English
      correct: false
    - text: No — only yes/no questions rise at the end in Ukrainian
      correct: true
    - text: No — Ukrainian questions always fall
      correct: false
    - text: No — Ukrainian has no intonation patterns
      correct: false
    explanation: In Ukrainian, only yes/no questions rise at the end. Questions with
      a question word rise on the question word then fall.
- type: anagram
  title: Unscramble the Word
  instruction: Rearrange the letters to form a Ukrainian word from this lesson.
  items:
  - scrambled: о д а в
    answer: вода
  - scrambled: о к о л о м
    answer: молоко
  - scrambled: а л о к ш
    answer: школа
  - scrambled: а к у р
    answer: рука
  - scrambled: а г о р о д
    answer: дорога
  - scrambled: а в а к
    answer: кава

```

---

## Research Notes

# Дослідження: Stress and Intonation

## State Standard Reference
§4.1.5: "Word stress" (lines 586, A1 phonetics)
§4.1.8: "Basic intonation patterns" (lines 596, A1 phonetics)
Alignment: This module directly addresses both §4.1.5 (free/mobile Ukrainian word stress) and §4.1.8 (declarative, interrogative, exclamatory intonation contours). Module 6 of A1.1 [First Contact] phase — foundational phonetic literacy before grammar study begins.

## Vocabulary Frequency

| Word | Notes | Key collocations |
|------|-------|------------------|
| замок | Minimal stress pair: зАмок (castle) vs замОк (lock). HIGH pedagogical value — same spelling, meaning changes with stress | зАмок у горах, замОк на дверях |
| вода | Top 300; last-syllable stress водА; stress shifts in genitive вОди → pedagogical preview | холодна вода, пити воду |
| рука | Top 200; mobile stress рукА → рУки (gen./pl.) — key example of mobile stress | права рука, моя рука |
| школа | Top 200; penultimate stress шкОла — common two-syllable pattern | моя школа, нова школа |
| молоко | Last-syllable stress молокО — three-syllable exemplar | свіже молоко |
| добрий | Top 100; first-syllable stress дОбрий — collocations: добрий день (greeting already known) | добрий день, добрий ранок |
| наголос | Metalanguage term; used to talk about stress in Ukrainian; not everyday vocabulary | наголос на першому складі |
| інтонація | Metalanguage; near-cognate with English "intonation" | правильна інтонація |
| питання | High-frequency; question intonation context | гарне питання |
| далеко | Last-syllable stress далекО; useful contrast with дОбрий (first-syllable) | дуже далеко |

## Cultural Hooks

1. **Stress-changes-meaning poem** (from textbook, Grade 3, Vashulenko p.69): The poem "Наголос — то зовсім не дрібниця..." directly models the pedagogical hook — "образи / образи / образи" (images / offenses / insults depending on stress). This ready-made, textbook-validated discovery artifact is perfect as the module's opening "noticing" moment.
2. **ЗАмок / замОк** minimal pair: Castles (замки) are a vivid feature of western Ukrainian landscape (Кам'янець-Подільський, Олеський замок). The pair zámok/zamók connects phonetics to real Ukrainian geography — culturally grounded and memorable.
3. **Intonation as social signal**: Ukrainian speakers recognize regional accents partly through intonation contours. The "musical" subtitle of the module ("The Music of Ukrainian") connects to the widely-held Ukrainian self-perception that their language is melodic — supported by multiple UNESCO references to Ukrainian as one of the most melodic Slavic languages.

## Common Learner Errors

1. **Applying English/Polish fixed-stress rules** → guessing final or penultimate syllable for every word — e.g., saying молОко (Polish-like penultimate) instead of молокО. Root cause: English word stress is partially predictable by syllable weight; Ukrainian has no such shortcut.
2. **Ignoring stress on minimal pairs** → saying замОк when meaning "castle" (зАмок). The textbook (Vashulenko, p.69) targets exactly this error with the "образи" poem — stress errors are meaning errors, not just pronunciation slips.
3. **Rising intonation on all questions** (English L1 transfer) → applying rising intonation to WH-questions (Де кафе? ДЕ кафе? — pitch should rise on the question word, then fall). English speakers tend to rise at sentence end; Ukrainian WH-questions fall after the question word.
4. **Flat/monotone reading** → not distinguishing declarative vs exclamatory contour. Це кафе. vs Це кафе! sound identical to untrained ears — exercises should isolate the pitch peak.

## Cross-References
- Builds on: a1-05 (Syllables and Word Division) — syllable boundaries are prerequisite for marking stress placement
- Prepares for: a1-07 (The Gender Code) — noun endings carry stress in some declension paradigms; a2-stress (§4.1.2) which covers stress shifts in verb conjugation and noun declension in full

## Teaching Strategy

- **Discovery exercise**: Present three words on screen: мАма, школА, молокО. Ask learner: "Where does the stress fall?" They observe: different words → different positions. Then show: зАмок — "a castle" and замОк — "a lock." Same letters, different meaning. *The rule reveals itself: Ukrainian stress is free and must be learned per word.* Mirrors the Vashulenko Grade 3 exercise (p.68-69) which uses the poem "образи / образи / образи" as the noticing anchor.
- **Best context**: Dictionary reading — stress marks (´) appear in dictionaries; learners who plan to use Ukrainian dictionaries need to read the acute accent correctly from the start. Also: reading signs and menus (Café contexts introduced in earlier modules).
- **Contrastive pairs**: зАмок ↔ замОк (castle vs lock); мУка ↔ мукА (torment vs flour); рукА ↔ рУки (hand nom. vs gen.pl.). For intonation: Це кафе. ↔ Це кафе? ↔ Це кафе! (same words, three sentence types).

## Notes for Content Writing
- This is Module 6 (pre-verbal phase): NO conjugated verb forms, NO imperatives. All Ukrainian examples must be bare nouns, noun phrases, or Це + noun. Classroom instructions ("Listen carefully", "Read aloud") in English only.
- The textbook poem on p.69 ("Наголос — то зовсім не дрібниця") is an ideal opener quotation — it's Grade 3 level, simple vocabulary, and directly states the pedagogical message.
- Stress marks in the module MUST use the acute accent (´) over the stressed vowel, not bold or caps — this matches dictionary convention learners will encounter.
- The "mobile stress" section should be framed as PREVIEW only — "awareness goal" per plan. Do not drill declension/conjugation stress shifts yet.
- Intonation section: use dialogue-like blockquote containers showing Це кафе. / Це кафе? / Це кафе! as the clearest possible contrast. No verbs needed.
- Section headers must be bilingual (M1-14 pattern): e.g., `## Наголос — Stress`, `## Інтонація — Intonation`.

## Your Task: Plan Adherence + Factual Verification

### PART A: Plan Adherence Check (MANDATORY)

The plan YAML above is the source of truth for what the module MUST contain. Check EVERY point systematically:

#### A1. Content Outline Points

For EACH section in `content_outline`, and for EACH `points` entry within that section:
1. Search the module content for evidence that this point is addressed
2. Classify as:
   - **COVERED** — the point is clearly addressed in the content. Quote the relevant sentence(s).
   - **MISSING** — the point does not appear in the content. This is a **MEDIUM** severity issue.
   - **PARTIAL** — some aspect is covered but key details are missing. Note what's missing.

**Output a checklist:**
```
### Plan Point Coverage
- Section "Вступ — Introduction":
  - [x] Point 1: "A1 immersion 10-50%..." — COVERED (line ~5: "...")
  - [ ] Point 2: "Cultural hook: Cyrillic script..." — MISSING
  ...
```

#### A2. Required Vocabulary

For EACH word in `vocabulary_hints.required`:
1. Search the module content (not just the word list section — the entire prose)
2. Check if it appears in the activities YAML (if provided in the content above)
3. Classify as:
   - **PRESENT** — found in prose AND activities
   - **PROSE ONLY** — in prose but not in any activity (LOW)
   - **MISSING** — not in prose at all (**HIGH** severity)

#### A3. Activity Hints

For EACH entry in `activity_hints`:
1. Check if an activity of that `type` exists
2. Check if the `focus` matches
3. Check if the `items` count is met
4. Flag missing activity types as **MEDIUM**

#### A4. Section Word Budgets

For EACH section in `content_outline` with a `words` target:
1. Estimate the word count of the corresponding content section
2. Flag sections >10% under their target as **MEDIUM**

#### A5. Pronunciation Videos (if plan has `pronunciation_videos`)

Check that video links from the plan are embedded in the content. Flag missing embeds as **MEDIUM**.

---

### PART B: Factual Verification

For EVERY grammar explanation, factual claim, cultural assertion, and historical reference in the module content:

1. **Search the reference sources above** for confirming or contradicting evidence
2. **Classify each claim** as one of:
   - **CONFIRMED [Tier 1]** — matches a reference source. Cite the source (chunk ID or source identifier) and quote both the module claim and the reference.
   - **DISCREPANCY [Tier 1]** — contradicts a reference source. HIGH severity. Cite both the module claim and the contradicting reference with chunk ID. Suggest the correct version.
   - **UNVERIFIED** — no reference coverage. Flag explicitly. Do NOT evaluate from your own knowledge. Do NOT mark as correct or incorrect — just "UNVERIFIED".

### Citation Enforcement

Every DISCREPANCY entry MUST include:
- Exact quote from the module content
- Exact quote from the reference source
- **Chunk ID or source identifier** (e.g., "Textbook, Grade 3, chunk_abc123" or "VESUM: lemma X")

Free-text citations with no chunk ID or source identifier carry ZERO weight and will be programmatically discarded.

### Special Checks

1. **Callout boxes** (`[!did-you-know]`, `[!myth-buster]`, `[!culture-note]`, `[!fun-fact]`): These are the #1 source of fabrication. Scrutinize every claim.
2. **Grammar rules**: Verify rule statements against textbook references. Flag overgeneralizations.
3. **Etymologies**: Verify word origins against references. Flag folk etymologies.
4. **Statistics and dates**: Flag unsourced numbers.
5. **VESUM failures**: Words not found in VESUM may be misspellings or Russianisms.

---

```
## Confirmed Claims
```

If you find fixable discrepancies, also output FIND/REPLACE pairs:


---

## Boundaries

- Do NOT evaluate style, naturalness, or pedagogy — that is Pass 2's job
- Do NOT score dimensions (except Plan Adherence and Factual Alignment) — that is Pass 2's job
- Do NOT evaluate factual claims from your own knowledge — ONLY compare against provided references
- Plan adherence checking uses the plan YAML as ground truth — this is objective, not subjective
- Do NOT fabricate references — if no reference covers a claim, mark it UNVERIFIED
- Maximum 15 FIND/REPLACE pairs (prioritize MISSING plan points over minor factual fixes)
